title: Carved cherry salad fork
subtitle: A three-tine green-wood salad fork to pair with a serving spoon
excerpt: A three-tine green-wood salad fork in cherry, carved to pair with a serving spoon. The tines are the new element: each one is carved separately with stop cuts at the base and slicing cuts along the length. Cherry carves cleanly green and the colour deepens to a warm red-brown over months of use.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III (1885) for green-wood fork proportions and tine-carving method; early twentieth-century Scandinavian spoon-carving tradition for the axe-to-knife sequence.

content.text: Carving a fork tines teaches the two cuts that most knife-only projects do not require: the
stop cut
to set the tine base, and the
slicing cut

to thin the tine shafts without splitting them. A three-tine salad fork is manageable for an intermediate carver; a five-tine fork requires the same techniques applied to a more delicate layout. This project uses
green wood
cherry, which is straight-grained and stiff enough to hold narrow tines without splitting as it dries.
The wood

A freshly riven cherry billet: 32 cm long, 6 cm across the widest face, 3 cm deep. Cherry is denser than sycamore and holds narrow tines better as the wood dries. It is food-safe and carves well green, though harder than sycamore or ash. Ash is the most forgiving alternative for a first fork; straight grain, easy to carve, food-safe. Avoid fruit-wood with knots or wavy grain in the tine area; a knot in a tine will break it.
Tools
A carving axe for roughing the
blank

, a sloyd knife for the handle and tine shaping, and a detail knife for the fine work between the tines. A leather strop. Raw linseed oil for finishing. The detail knife edge is the most critical: if it is not sharp enough to slice cleanly between the tines without tearing, the tines will split.
Method
Work with push cuts as the default. At the tine stage, switch to stop cuts and slicing cuts. Strop every 15 minutes.

Draw the fork outline on the blank: a 24 cm handle, a 6 cm tine section. Mark three tines, each 8 mm wide at the base and 5 mm wide at the tip, evenly spaced with 5 mm gaps between them. The tines run parallel with the grain; this is not optional; tines that cross the grain will break.

Rough the profile with the carving axe: remove the waste on either side of the handle and square up the tine block. Leave the tine block 1 cm deeper than the finished tine thickness at this stage; you will thin it later.
Shape the handle with the sloyd knife. Push cuts along the handle, thumb-pivot cuts at the neck. The handle cross-section is oval.

Set the tine base with stop cuts. Place the sloyd knife perpendicular to the grain at the base of the tine section and press straight down; this is a stop cut, setting the boundary line. Make the same stop cut on both flat faces and both side edges of the tine block.

Remove the waste between the tines using the detail knife with slicing cuts: angle the blade at 30 degrees to the wood surface, draw it through the gap with steady arm pressure. Work from the tip of the tine toward the base stop cut. The slicing angle reduces resistance on the cherry grain. The gap between the tines should be 5 mm wide and consistent top to bottom.

Thin the tines to 4 mm thickness with push cuts on the sloyd knife, working from tip toward base. Check each tine by holding the fork up to the light: the tines should be consistent in thickness and taper very slightly toward the tip. A tine that is clearly thinner on one face than the other will twist as it dries.

Round the tine tips with the detail knife: three or four push cuts around the circumference, blade angle varying to follow the curve. Keep tine tips blunt for a fork that will not pierce food unintentionally.
Dry the fork flat for 3 to 4 weeks. Cherry dries slowly compared to ash; the tines are at risk if the drying rate is too fast, so keep the fork away from radiators and out of direct sun.
Finishing

Sand through 120 and 180 grit once dry; sand inside the tine gaps with a folded strip of sandpaper. Apply raw linseed oil to all surfaces, let it soak for an hour, wipe off surplus. The oil needs 2 weeks to cure before the fork is food-safe. Cherry's colour deepens to a warm amber after a few months of use and oiling.
Variations
Ash: more forgiving to carve, lighter, food-safe. The alternative for a first fork.
Five-tine version: the same method with two additional tines, each 6 mm wide. Requires a finer detail knife.
Care
Wash promptly after use and towel-dry. Never soak in water; the tines are the most vulnerable part. Re-oil when the wood looks dry, roughly monthly in regular use.

attrs.intro: Common problems with the salad fork.

items.fix: Keep tines 5 mm thick minimum until the fork is fully dry. Carve only along the grain. A split tine cannot be repaired; if it splits at the base, cut it off and work with two tines.
items.cause: Cutting across the grain in the tine section, or tine is too thin before drying

items.fix: Mark the gap centre line in pencil before slicing. Remove waste from both sides of the gap toward the centre line rather than in one pass.
items.cause: Slicing cuts angled inconsistently across the gap

items.fix: Check tine wall thickness before drying and equalise. A tine that is 6 mm on one face and 3 mm on the other will twist. Cannot be corrected after drying.
items.cause: Tine walls uneven in thickness; one face thinner than the other
items.symptom: Tines twist as the wood dries

attrs.heading: What can go wrong
